description: System health endpoint queries Alert, SystemMetric, AiService, WorkflowJob, and ScheduledPost without any tenant scoping. Alert and SystemMetric models have no tenantId field, which is by design for global system data. However, WorkflowJob and ScheduledPost DO have tenant-relevant data (via channelId chains). The endpoint counts ALL queued jobs and ALL scheduled posts across all tenants.
intended_behavior: Health endpoint should show only data relevant to the authenticated tenant's resources (jobs, posts) while system-level metrics (CPU, RAM) remain global.
actual_behavior: All counts are global across tenants. workflowJob.count and scheduledPost.count include data from all tenants.
proposed_fix: For workflowJob and scheduledPost counts, add tenant scoping by first fetching tenant channel IDs, then filtering: `where: { status: { in: ['queued', 'running'] }, channelId: { in: tenantChannelIds } }`.

description: Alerts GET endpoint has no tenant scoping. The Alert model has no tenantId field. This is by design since alerts are system-level. However, in a multi-tenant deployment, one tenant can see alerts created by system operations triggered by another tenant.
intended_behavior: For a self-hosted single-tenant deployment, this is acceptable. For multi-tenant, alerts should be filterable by source/scope.
actual_behavior: All alerts are visible to all authenticated users regardless of tenant.
proposed_fix: Low priority for self-hosted deployment. Document as known multi-tenant gap. If needed, add an optional `tenantId` field to Alert model.

description: Snooze endpoint sets status to 'suppressed' and returns a calculated `snoozedUntil` timestamp, but the snoozedUntil time is never persisted to the database. The Alert model has no `snoozedUntil` field. There is no mechanism to automatically un-suppress the alert after the snooze duration expires.
intended_behavior: The alert should be suppressed for the specified duration and automatically return to 'open' status when the snooze period expires.
actual_behavior: The alert is permanently set to 'suppressed' status. The `snoozedUntil` time is returned to the client but never stored. No scheduled job or cron exists to un-suppress alerts after the duration.
proposed_fix: 1. Add a `snoozedUntil DateTime?` field to the Alert model. 2. Store the calculated snooze end time in the database. 3. Add a repeatable maintenance job or modify the existing metrics job to check for expired snoozes and reset them to 'open'.

description: The inner catch block at line 38 that handles body parsing failure is silent - it doesn't log the error. While this is intentional to handle the no-body case (using default duration), it could hide JSON parse errors.
intended_behavior: Silently fall back to default duration when no body is provided.
actual_behavior: Also silently swallows actual JSON parse errors (e.g., malformed JSON body), making debugging harder.
proposed_fix: Add a debug-level log: `// body parse failed, using default` or check if the request has a content-type header before attempting parse.

description: Acknowledge-all endpoint acknowledges ALL open alerts across all tenants. In a multi-tenant deployment, one tenant's admin can acknowledge alerts triggered by another tenant's operations.
intended_behavior: Each tenant should only be able to acknowledge alerts relevant to their operations.
actual_behavior: updateMany with `where: { status: 'open' }` affects all open alerts globally.
proposed_fix: Low priority for self-hosted single-tenant deployment. For multi-tenant, would need Alert.tenantId or source-based filtering.

description: Affiliate products GET endpoint intentionally skips tenant scoping (per the NOTE comment at line 32 referencing KI-020). However, this means ALL products across all tenants are visible to any authenticated user, including competitors' product data and commission rates.
intended_behavior: Products are described as global entities, but this leaks sensitive business data (commission rates, sales angles, revenue) between tenants.
actual_behavior: All affiliate products are returned to any authenticated user regardless of tenant affiliation. The comment says this is to avoid hiding newly created products not yet assigned to pools, but this is a broad exception.
proposed_fix: Add optional tenant scoping: show products that either (a) are in a channel pool owned by this tenant, or (b) were created within the last N hours (grace period for unassigned products). Alternatively, filter to products with at least one channel pool linked to the tenant.

description: The tenant ownership check on GET has a logic flaw. The condition at line 48 is `if (ctx.tenantId && product.channelPools.length > 0)`. If a product has NO channel pools (length === 0), the tenant check is skipped entirely, allowing any tenant to view any product detail. The inner check at line 49 `product.channelPools.some(pool => pool.channel != null)` only checks if the channel was included (it always will be due to the include), not if the channel belongs to the tenant.
intended_behavior: Only the tenant that owns the product (via channel pool chain) should be able to view its details.
actual_behavior: Products with no channel pools bypass the tenant check. Products with channel pools use a flawed `pool.channel != null` check that always passes (the include ensures channel is populated), then falls through to a secondary DB query that correctly checks tenant ownership. The first check at line 49 is always true, so the secondary check at line 52-58 is never reached.
proposed_fix: Remove the flawed `ownsProduct` check. Always perform the authoritative DB query: `const tenantPool = await ctx.db.channelAffiliatePool.findFirst({ where: { affiliateProductId: id, channel: { socialAccount: { emailAccount: { tenantId: ctx.tenantId } } } } })`. If no pool exists for this tenant, return notFound.

description: The PUT handler tenant check at line 103 uses `if (ctx.tenantId && existing.id)` - `existing.id` is always truthy (it's a UUID string), making it a redundant condition.
intended_behavior: Check tenant ownership before allowing update.
actual_behavior: The check `existing.id` is always true - it should just be `if (ctx.tenantId)`.
proposed_fix: Simplify to `if (ctx.tenantId) {`.

description: Affiliate links GET endpoint has no tenant scoping. It returns all affiliate products with shortUrls across all tenants.
intended_behavior: Should only return links for products associated with the authenticated tenant's channels.
actual_behavior: Returns all products with non-null shortUrl regardless of tenant.
proposed_fix: Add tenant scoping by joining through channelPools -> channel -> socialAccount -> emailAccount chain, or filter products to those with at least one channel pool owned by the tenant.

description: Affiliate links POST endpoint creates/updates shortUrl on any product without verifying the product belongs to the authenticated tenant.
intended_behavior: Should only allow creating links for products associated with the tenant's channels.
actual_behavior: Any authenticated non-viewer user can create a short link for any product in the system by providing its productId.
proposed_fix: After finding the product, verify tenant ownership via channelAffiliatePool chain before updating.

description: The redirect endpoint uses `getDb()` instead of `ctx.db`. This is a public endpoint (no auth), so `ctx` is not available, which is the correct pattern for unauthenticated endpoints. However, this means the endpoint bypasses tenant isolation.
intended_behavior: Public redirect endpoint correctly uses getDb() since there's no auth context. This is acceptable for a public redirect.
actual_behavior: Uses getDb() as expected for a public/unauthenticated endpoint. The comment in the rules says workers MAY use getDb(), and this is analogous. No real issue here.
proposed_fix: No fix needed - this is the correct pattern for a public endpoint. However, consider adding rate limiting to prevent abuse (click fraud, DoS).

description: Public redirect endpoint has no rate limiting. An attacker could inflate click counts via automated requests, or use this as a DoS vector.
intended_behavior: Public endpoint should have rate limiting to prevent click fraud and abuse.
actual_behavior: No rate limiting on this public endpoint. Unlimited requests can be made to inflate totalClicks counter.
proposed_fix: Add IP-based rate limiting (e.g., 100 requests per minute per IP). Consider deduplication by ipHash within a time window to prevent click fraud.

description: The click recording and totalClicks increment are in a Prisma $transaction, which is good. However, there's a TOCTOU issue: the product is looked up first (line 38), and then the transaction writes to it (line 62). Between the lookup and the transaction, the product could be deactivated or deleted by another request.
intended_behavior: Atomic click recording with consistent product state.
actual_behavior: The product lookup and the click recording are not in the same transaction. If the product is deleted between the two, the transaction will fail with a foreign key error (productId references a deleted product).
proposed_fix: Wrap the entire operation (lookup + click + increment) in a single interactive transaction, or handle the Prisma error for deleted product gracefully.

description: Storefronts GET endpoint attempts tenant scoping via `where.channel = { socialAccount: { emailAccount: { tenantId: ctx.tenantId } } }` (line 38-40). However, the Storefront Prisma model has NO `channel` relation defined - it has a `channelId` foreign key but no `channel Channel @relation(...)` line, and the Channel model has no `storefronts Storefront[]` back-relation. This query will fail at runtime with a Prisma error because `channel` is not a known relation on Storefront.
intended_behavior: Tenant scoping through channel -> socialAccount -> emailAccount -> tenantId chain.
actual_behavior: Prisma query will throw an error because the `channel` relation does not exist on the Storefront model. The endpoint will return a 500 error for all requests.
proposed_fix: 1. Add `channel Channel @relation(fields: [channelId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)` to the Storefront model in schema.prisma. 2. Add `storefronts Storefront[]` to the Channel model. 3. Run `prisma generate`. Alternatively, use a two-step approach: first fetch tenant channel IDs, then filter by `channelId: { in: tenantChannelIds }`.

description: Storefronts [id] GET endpoint performs tenant ownership check correctly using a separate channel query (lines 47-54), avoiding the missing relation issue. However, this is inconsistent with the list endpoint (storefronts/route.ts) which uses the broken `where.channel` approach.
intended_behavior: Consistent tenant scoping approach across list and detail endpoints.
actual_behavior: Detail endpoint works (uses channelId lookup), but list endpoint is broken (uses non-existent relation). Inconsistent patterns.
proposed_fix: Fix the list endpoint to use the same two-step approach as the detail endpoint, or fix the Prisma schema to add the missing relation.

description: The GET endpoint's serviceType filter validation uses `validServiceTypes = ['text_generation', 'image_generation', 'tts', 'embedding', 'moderation']` (line 42), but the POST endpoint's Zod schema uses `serviceType: z.enum(['text', 'image', 'video', 'voice'])` (line 11). The Prisma schema comment says `// text, image, video, voice`. Services are created with values like 'text' but the filter only accepts 'text_generation'. Filtering by serviceType in GET will never match any records created via POST.
intended_behavior: The filter values in GET should match the values used when creating services via POST.
actual_behavior: GET allows filtering by 'text_generation' etc. but services are stored with 'text' etc. These will never match.
proposed_fix: Change `validServiceTypes` in GET to `['text', 'image', 'video', 'voice']` to match the Zod schema and Prisma model values.

description: The GET endpoint's status filter validation uses `validStatuses = ['active', 'inactive', 'disabled', 'error']` (line 43), but the Prisma schema says `// active, degraded, down, disabled` and the UPDATE schema at ai-services/[id]/route.ts uses `z.enum(['active', 'degraded', 'down', 'disabled'])`. Values 'inactive' and 'error' are not valid status values; 'degraded' and 'down' are missing from the filter.
intended_behavior: Filter validation should accept the same status values that the model actually uses.
actual_behavior: GET allows filtering by 'inactive' and 'error' (which don't exist in the data) but not by 'degraded' and 'down' (which do exist).
proposed_fix: Change `validStatuses` in GET to `['active', 'degraded', 'down', 'disabled']` to match the Prisma model and update schema.

description: The GET endpoint's provider filter validation uses `validProviders = ['ollama', 'openai', 'comfyui', 'piper', 'elevenlabs', 'http']`. If new providers are added to the system, they won't be filterable until this hardcoded list is updated. This is a maintenance burden.
intended_behavior: Provider filter should accept any valid provider value.
actual_behavior: Hardcoded list may go stale as providers are added. However, this is a common pattern and low severity.
proposed_fix: Consider removing the validation and just passing through any provider value as a filter, or dynamically querying distinct provider values.

description: System errors endpoint (which queries Alert model with severity 'critical' or 'error') has no tenant scoping. Same issue as the alerts endpoint - Alert model has no tenantId.
intended_behavior: Show only errors relevant to the authenticated tenant.
actual_behavior: Shows all critical/error alerts across all tenants.
proposed_fix: Low priority for self-hosted deployment. Same as P2E_002.

description: Multiple places in the research worker parse AI-generated JSON responses without validating the shape. At line 68, `trends = JSON.parse(result.content)` assigns the parsed result directly to a typed variable without Zod validation. If the AI returns valid JSON but with wrong structure (e.g., missing fields, wrong types), subsequent code at line 87 will throw at runtime when accessing `trend.suggestedContentTypes.join(', ')`.
intended_behavior: Validate the shape of AI-generated JSON responses before using them.
actual_behavior: Raw JSON.parse output is trusted to match the expected type. A malformed response (e.g., missing `suggestedContentTypes` array) will cause a runtime error.
proposed_fix: Add Zod schema validation after JSON.parse for each handler (handleTrends, handleTopics, handlePopulateKnowledge). Wrap in try/catch with descriptive error messages.

description: The `startResearchWorker()` function adds a repeatable job `research:trends` every time it's called. If the worker restarts, BullMQ deduplicates repeatable jobs by their repeat options, so this is safe. However, there's no cleanup of stale repeatable jobs if the repeat interval changes between deployments.
intended_behavior: Repeatable job should be updated when configuration changes.
actual_behavior: BullMQ handles deduplication, so this is low severity. Old repeatable config persists in Redis until manually cleaned.
proposed_fix: Consider calling `researchQueue.removeRepeatableByKey()` before adding to ensure clean state on restart.

description: The backup handler uses `process.env.DATABASE_URL` directly in a shell command via `execFileAsync('sh', ['-c', ...])`. While `execFile` is safer than `exec`, the `-c` flag passes the string to `sh` for interpretation. If DATABASE_URL contains shell metacharacters (e.g., in the password), this could cause command injection or backup failure.
intended_behavior: Safely execute pg_dump with the database URL.
actual_behavior: The database URL is interpolated into a shell command string. While passwords with special characters like `$`, backticks, or semicolons are quoted with double quotes, shell still interprets `$` and backticks inside double quotes.
proposed_fix: Use single quotes around the URL in the shell command: `pg_dump '${dbUrl}'`, or better, pass DATABASE_URL as an environment variable to the child process instead of embedding it in the command string. Best approach: use execFileAsync('pg_dump', [dbUrl]) piped to gzip separately.

description: The backup handler has a hardcoded fallback database URL: `'postgresql://airevstream:airevstream_dev@localhost:5432/airevstream'`. While this is a development default, it exposes credentials in source code.
intended_behavior: Should require DATABASE_URL to be set, or fail gracefully if not configured.
actual_behavior: Falls back to hardcoded development credentials if DATABASE_URL is not set.
proposed_fix: Remove the fallback and throw an error if DATABASE_URL is not set: `const dbUrl = process.env.DATABASE_URL; if (!dbUrl) throw new Error('DATABASE_URL not configured');`

description: The 'ram_used_gb' metric is created with `unit: 'bytes'` but the value is calculated in gigabytes (`(totalMem - freeMem) / (1024 ** 3)`). The unit label is misleading.
intended_behavior: Unit should match the actual value unit.
actual_behavior: Value is in GB but unit is labeled 'bytes'.
proposed_fix: Change `unit: 'bytes'` to `unit: 'GB'`.

description: The SSE pollAlerts function at line 24 queries alerts without tenant scoping. This is consistent with the REST alerts endpoint and acceptable for the same reasons (Alert model has no tenantId).
intended_behavior: Alert polling should be scoped to relevant tenant data.
actual_behavior: All alerts are broadcast to all connected SSE clients regardless of tenant.
proposed_fix: Low priority - consistent with REST endpoint design. Same as P2E_002.

description: The SSE endpoint pre-fetches tenant channel IDs once at connection time (line 165-169) and uses them for the lifetime of the connection. If channels are added or removed while the SSE connection is active, the polling will use stale channel IDs, potentially missing new channels or including deleted ones.
intended_behavior: Channel IDs should stay current throughout the SSE connection.
actual_behavior: Channel IDs are cached at connection start and never refreshed. Long-lived connections (hours/days) will have increasingly stale tenant channel lists.
proposed_fix: Periodically refresh tenant channel IDs (e.g., every 5 minutes) within the SSE polling loop. Or refresh on every Nth cycle.

description: The SSE polling updates `lastCheck = new Date()` at line 204 after every poll cycle, regardless of whether an event was found. This means if a poller cycle finds no events, the next cycle's `lastCheck` will be set to the current time, potentially missing events that were created between the previous lastCheck and now but were not checked by this specific poller type (since pollers rotate cyclically).
intended_behavior: Each poller type should track its own lastCheck independently so rotating through pollers doesn't cause missed events.
actual_behavior: A single shared `lastCheck` is updated after every cycle. With 4 pollers and 10-second intervals, each poller type only runs every 40 seconds. If poller A runs at T=0 and sets lastCheck=T0, then pollers B, C, D run and advance lastCheck to T+10, T+20, T+30. When poller A runs again at T=40, it checks for events created after T+30, missing any events created between T+0 and T+30.
proposed_fix: Maintain per-poller-type lastCheck timestamps: `const lastChecks = new Map<string, Date>()`. Each poller reads/writes its own lastCheck entry.

description: The revenue endpoint accepts `start` and `end` date strings from query params but doesn't validate their format beyond `new Date(d)` and `isNaN(d.getTime())`. Unusual date strings that parse to unexpected values (e.g., '2024-13-01') may produce unexpected results.
intended_behavior: Date parameters should be validated as proper ISO date strings.
actual_behavior: Any string that `new Date()` can parse is accepted, including ambiguous formats.
proposed_fix: Add explicit date format validation (e.g., regex for ISO 8601) or use Zod's z.string().datetime() for query param validation.

description: Storefronts POST endpoint verifies that the provided channelId belongs to the tenant (lines 104-113), which is correct. However, if `ctx.tenantId` is falsy (which shouldn't happen in practice but is coded as a conditional), the channel ownership check is skipped.
intended_behavior: Always verify channel ownership before creating a storefront.
actual_behavior: The tenant check is conditional on `ctx.tenantId` being truthy. If ctx.tenantId is somehow null/undefined, any channelId would be accepted.
proposed_fix: Remove the tenantId conditional and always perform the ownership check, or validate that ctx.tenantId is always present after authenticate().

description: The flat values conversion at lines 53-59 only converts 'queue_depth' to 'queueDepth' camelCase. Other snake_case metric types like 'ram_used_gb' would not be converted to camelCase in the flat output.
intended_behavior: All metric types should be consistently cased in the flat output.
actual_behavior: Only 'queue_depth' is converted. 'ram_used_gb' would appear as-is in the flat object.
proposed_fix: Add a general snake_case to camelCase converter, or explicitly handle all known snake_case keys.

description: The validMetricTypes array at line 15 includes `['cpu', 'ram', 'disk', 'network', 'queue_depth', 'sessions']` but the maintenance worker at maintenance.worker.ts line 153 also creates 'ram_used_gb' metrics. This type is not in the validMetricTypes list, so it won't appear in the 'all metrics' view (only available via the specific type filter if the user knows the key).
intended_behavior: All metric types created by the system should be queryable via the metrics endpoint.
actual_behavior: The 'ram_used_gb' metric type is created by the maintenance worker but not included in validMetricTypes, so it's excluded from the default all-metrics view.
proposed_fix: Add 'ram_used_gb' to the validMetricTypes array.

description: The research endpoint catches Zod parse errors and returns a generic 'Invalid request body' message at line 43, but also catches ALL errors in this inner try/catch (e.g., req.json() failure for non-JSON content type). This is acceptable behavior but loses the specific Zod validation messages.
intended_behavior: Return specific validation error messages to help the client fix their request.
actual_behavior: Returns generic 'Invalid request body. Expected type: trends, topics, or knowledge-update' for both JSON parse errors and Zod validation failures.
proposed_fix: Separate JSON parse errors from Zod validation errors. Use `safeParse` instead of `parse` and return specific field-level error messages from Zod.

description: The handleTrends function uses createMany to bulk-insert knowledge base entries without checking for duplicates. If the same trend topics are generated on consecutive runs (every 12 hours), duplicate entries will accumulate in the knowledge base.
intended_behavior: Avoid duplicate knowledge base entries for the same trends.
actual_behavior: Each 12-hour run creates new entries even if the same topics were identified in previous runs. Over time, this leads to data bloat.
proposed_fix: Before creating, check for existing entries with matching domain='platform_ops', category='trends', and similar titles. Use upsert or skipDuplicates.

description: The redirect endpoint matches products by `shortUrl: { endsWith: '/' + shortCode }`. If two products have shortUrls that both end with the same shortCode (e.g., 'https://link1.example.com/abc123' and 'https://link2.example.com/abc123'), findFirst will return an arbitrary match.
intended_behavior: Each shortCode should uniquely identify a product.
actual_behavior: findFirst returns the first match found by the database, which may not be deterministic if multiple products share the same shortCode suffix.
proposed_fix: Add a unique index on the shortCode portion of the shortUrl, or store the shortCode as a separate indexed column. Alternatively, use findMany and check for ambiguous matches.

description: Health and metrics endpoints use `authenticateAny` (JWT or API key) while alerts and errors endpoints use `authenticate` (JWT only). This inconsistency means API key users can check health/metrics but cannot manage alerts.
intended_behavior: Consistent authentication approach across system endpoints, or documented intentional differences.
actual_behavior: Mixed auth approaches - health/metrics accept API keys, alerts/errors require JWT only.
proposed_fix: Document the intentional difference, or standardize to authenticateAny for all read-only system endpoints.
